text: The surgeon begins the cystectomy by making a peritoneal incision in the Douglas pouch. Using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection, the seminal vesicles are carefully separated from the Denonvilliers' fascia. The fascia is then incised, allowing the posterior surface of the prostate to be dissected away from the perirectal tissue.
The surgeon isolates and cuts the vas deferens on both sides. Seminal vesicles are dissected from surrounding tissue until reaching their junction with the prostate and posterior bladder neck.

The surgeon isolates the ureters by excising the peritoneum over the iliac artery. Each ureter is carefully dissected from surrounding tissue, preserving periureteral tissue to prevent ischemia. The ureters are dissected up to the uretero-vesicle junction, clipped twice, and transected. A frozen section from the distal end is sent to pathology, and the procedure is repeated on the opposite side.

The surgeon extends the incision alongside the lateral border of the median umbilical ligament. Using sharp and blunt dissection, the lateral wall of the bladder is separated from the pelvic side wall. The umbilical artery is clipped twice at its origin and transected with a ligature. The pedicle is controlled by repeated application of LigaSure and sharp dissection with scissors.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the prostate and bladder from the rectum, then dissects the retzius space anteriorly, allowing the bladder to be dropped from the abdominal wall. The Alexis port at the stoma site is visible in the upper right corner of the screen, indicating its use for safe insufflation and future urinary diversion.

The surgeon dissects the periprostatic fatty tissue, exposing the dorsal venous complex. Sharp dissection of the dorsal venous complex is performed, with minimal bleeding observed due to pneumoperitoneum pressure. The dorsal venous complex is completely transected, and vascular control is achieved using an A-III-Zero monofilament suture ligature.

The surgeon dissects the prostatic apex, fully exposing the urethra. The urethra is clipped twice to ensure control before transection, with precautions taken to prevent urine spillage. A frozen section is obtained from the apical urethra for pathological examination.

The surgical specimen is placed into a retrieval bag. The surgeon prepares to remove the specimen through a small phantasteel incision, ensuring the bag is tightly closed with a clip to prevent spillage.

The surgeon transfers the left ureter beneath the colon to the right side. A segment of ileum, approximately twenty centimeters from the ileocecal valve, is marked and divided using staplers. The continuity of the ileum is restored by performing a side-to-side anastomosis with two applications of sixty millimeter staplers.

The surgeon closes the side opening of the anastomosis using a stapler. The left ureter is opened and spatulated, followed by an incision at the lateral border of the ileum for the uretero-ileal anastomosis. The ureter is then attached to the ileal wall using a 4-0 PDS suture, employing a novel technique.

The surgeon brings the distal end of the isolated ileal segment out of the body using the Alexis port. A metal suction cannula is inserted into the ileum and brought out through the proximal ileal incision. A ureter catheter, loaded on a guide wire, is inserted into the ureter and guided out of the ileal stoma without additional punctures. The ureteral catheter is fixed to the ileum using A-V-O rapid vicryl suture, minimizing the risk of urine leakage.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis with continuous sutures on both sides using 4-0 PDS sutures. The same procedure is repeated for the right ureter. After completing the uretero-ileal anastomosis on both sides, the distal end of the ileum is brought out of the abdomen through the Alexis port and matured into a stoma.

This novel method, reduces the time for anastomosis and ileal stoma creation, by eliminating the clumsy manipulation of guide wires or ureter catheters in a floppy ileum. It also avoids further risk of urine leakage, which may be a concern if the catheters are brought out by stab wounds through ileum and abdominal wall.
